comments.text: I would like to report some computational evidence concerning the case \(k=3\). For a squarefree integer \(r\leq n\), define\[ w_n(r)=\max\{m\leq n:\operatorname{rad}(m)=r\}. \]Thus, once a radical \(r\) is chosen, the best representative is the largest integer \(m\leq n\) having that radical. With this notation, \(G(n)\) is the value of the following \(0\)-\(1\) integer programme:\[ \max \sum_r w_n(r)x_r, \]subject to\[ \sum_{r:p\mid r}x_r\leq 1 \qquad (p\leq n\ \text{prime}), \]where\[ x_r\in\{0,1\}. \]Here \(r\) ranges over all radicals occurring among integers \(m\leq n\). The radical \(r=1\) is harmless: it is unconstrained and is always selected. Indeed, replacing any chosen integer by the largest integer with the same radical preserves admissibility and weakly increases the objective. The constraints simply express the condition that no prime may divide two chosen radicals. Let \(G_{\leq 2}(n)\) denote the value of the same optimization problem, but with the additional restriction\[ \omega(r)\leq 2. \]Thus \(G_{\leq 2}(n)\) is the best value obtainable using only integers with at most two distinct prime factors, namely prime powers and numbers of the form \(p^a q^b\). If\[ G(n)=G_{\leq 2}(n), \]then there exists an optimal admissible set containing no integer with three distinct prime factors. Of course, this does not exclude the possibility that other optimal sets may contain such integers. The exact algorithm used was as follows. Given \(n\) and \(t\in\{2,\infty\}\), initialize \({\cal R}=\emptyset\). For each \(m=1,\ldots,n\), compute\[ r=\operatorname{rad}(m). \]If \(t=2\) and \(\omega(r)>2\), discard \(m\). Otherwise, for each radical \(r\), retain only the largest integer \(m\leq n\) with that radical; call this value \(w(r)\). Then solve\[ \max \sum_{r\in{\cal R}} w(r)x_r \]subject to\[ \sum_{\substack{r\in{\cal R}\\ p\mid r}}x_r\leq 1 \qquad(p\leq n\ \text{prime}), \]with\[ x_r\in\{0,1\}. \]The case \(t=\infty\) gives \(G(n)\), while the case \(t=2\) gives \(G_{\leq 2}(n)\). The computations were carried out with the assistance of ChatGPT 5.5, which was used to formulate and run the integer-programming calculations and to help check the resulting values. The exact computations give\[ \begin{array}{c|c|c|c} n & G(n) & G_{\leq 2}(n) & G(n)-G_{\leq 2}(n)\\ \hline 20 & 98 & 98 & 0\\ 50 & 445 & 445 & 0\\ 100 & 1355 & 1355 & 0\\ 200 & 5104 & 5104 & 0\\ 500 & 24914 & 24914 & 0\\ 1000 & 85684 & 85684 & 0\\ 2000 & 302545 & 302545 & 0\\ 5000 & 1636900 & 1636900 & 0\\ 10000 & 5972789 & 5972789 & 0\\ 20000 & 21825994 & 21825994 & 0 \end{array} \]Thus, for every tested value of \(n\leq 20000\), the full optimum is already attained within the subfamily of integers having at most two distinct prime factors. In particular, although integers with three or more distinct prime factors are available, they do not improve the optimum in these computations. This is only finite computational evidence, and therefore not a disproof of any eventual statement. However, it suggests the possible counter-conjecture\[ G(n)=G_{\leq 2}(n) \]for infinitely many \(n\), perhaps even for every \(n\). Equivalently, the weighted matching problem using only prime powers and two-prime radicals may already be strong enough to absorb all possible gains from using radicals with three or more distinct prime factors.
